export_meta.task_description: Identify each laser welding experimental record in the following passage. For each record, extract the welding process parameters, including welding power (W), welding speed (m/min), defocusing distance (mm), shielding gas, and shielding gas flow rate(L/min), together with the corresponding mechanical performance metrics, including tensile strength, yield strength, and elongation rate (%). Only extract information explicitly stated in the passage.
export_meta.source_filename: Microstructure and mechanical properties of laser welded Ti-6Al-4V (TC4)xxx.pdf

source.chunk:
Optics & Laser Technology 170 (2024) 110320
Available online 10 November 2023
0030-3992/© 2023 Elsevier Ltd.
All rights reserved.
Full length article
Microstructure and mechanical properties of laser welded Ti-6Al-4V (TC4)
titanium alloy joints
Guowei Li, Yinshuang Wang, Yahong Liang
, Pengxiang Gao, Xinyu Liu, Wencai Xu, Dawei Yang
School of Materials Science and Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot 010051, China
ARTICLE INFO
Keywords:
Laser beam welding
TC4 titanium alloy
Microstructure
Mechanical properties
ABSTRACT
Laser beam welding (LBW) was applied to join 3 mm thick TC4 titanium alloy plates in this study.
Three-factor
and three-level orthogonal experiments were designed to choose the best welding parameters through the
analysis of range and variance.
The microstructures of the welded joints were analyzed by optical microscopy
(OM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD).
The mechanical prop -
erties of the welded joints were analyzed by tensile tests.
The experimental results indicated that the defocused
position had the largest effect, followed by the laser power and welding speed.
The optimal welding parameters
were laser power 2.3 kW, welding speed 0.04 m/s and defocused position 0 mm.
The heat-affected zone (HAZ)
consisted of α ′ martensite, primary α phase ( α
) and primary β phase ( β
).
In the fusion zone (FZ), the acicular α ′
martensite was distributed in the β columnar grains.
The tensile strength of the laser welded joint under the
optimal welding parameters was 985 MPa, which is less than that of the base metal (BM).
The peak value of the
grain misorientation angle in the HAZ was related to the amount of martensite.
The laser welded joints with finer
martensite and equiaxed α + β would have better mechanical properties.
The grains in the HAZ had a preferred
orientation in the {0 0 1} direction.
1. Introduction
Titanium and its alloys became crucial engineering structural ma -
terials in the 1950s.
The usage of titanium and its alloys is now wide -
spread in the domains of medicine, aerospace, biomaterials,
biomedicine and marine engineering due to their low density, high
strength-to-weight ratio, excellent resistance to corrosion, fine resis -
tance to plastic deformation, and low-temperature brittleness [1 – 4] .
With the rapid development of the titanium industry, processes for
welding titanium alloy have developed swiftly in recent decades to
lower welding costs while enhancing weld stability, quality and effi -
ciency [5,6] .
Commonly used welding methods for titanium alloys include tung -
sten inert gas (TIG) welding, brazing, electron beam welding (EBW),
LBW and other welding methods.
Every welding technique has advan -
tages and disadvantages.
Due to its low weld strength and production
efficiency, brazing cannot meet the welding requirements of large-scale
machines.
Nonetheless, the residual stress and deformation can be
improved during brazing and precision forming of the complex parts can
be achieved [7] .
Liu et al.
[8] investigated the brazing of ZrO
ceramic to
TC4 titanium alloy.

source.chunk:
They found that amorphous brazing could reduce
residual stress and improve the mechanical properties of welded joints.
EBW is performed under vacuum conditions, which can protect the
molten metal from environmental contaminants.
However, the equip -
ment is expensive, and it can be challenging to achieve vacuum condi -
tions [9] .
A study on the EBW of TC4 titanium alloy sheets was
conducted by Fan et al [10] .
The electron beam pulsed amplitude was
changed to improve the surface forming of the weld seam, thereby
improving the mechanical properties of the welded joints.
TIG welding
is a flexible technique and has a comparatively low cost.
However, as the
increment of heat input is generated by TIG welding, the welded joints
can become distorted, and the width of HAZ can become wider. More -
over, the mechanical properties of welded joints are also affected
[11,12] .
He et al.
[13] measured the residual stress of TIG welded joints
by the small hole detection method.
According to the findings, the re -
sidual stress and the weld width increased with increasing heat input.
Compared with other welding techniques, LBW has the characteristics of
flexible operation, high energy density and welding efficiency [14,15] .
Emre E H et al.
[16] studied the effects of different welding speeds on the
mechanical properties of a 1.2 mm thick biomedical TC4 titanium alloy

Optics and Laser Technology 170 (2024) 110320
and reported that the maximum tensile strength was obtained at 250
cm/min welding speed for laser welded joints.
Chamanfar A et al. [17]
found that the ultimate tensile strength of Ti1023 titanium alloy laser
welded joints was 84 % of that of the BM.
The fracture surfaces of the
tensile samples indicated a mixed brittle and ductile fracture mode. Guo
et al.
[18] researched underwater laser welding of a 2 mm thick TC4
titanium alloy and found the near-FZ consisted of the α + α ′ phases,
whereas the near-BM contained almost no α phases.
Malikov A G et al.
[19] analyzed the microstructure of the laser welded joints of titanium
alloy and aluminum alloy, the analysis showed the presence of brittle
and gliding fractures.
Mohamad A et al.
[20] explored the laser welding
of Ti6Al4V titanium alloy in air and a water medium.
They found the
distribution of martensite was basket-like in the underwater-welded
joints, which was different from the air-welded joints.
It is infrequent
to use orthogonal experiments to obtain the optimal welding parameters
of laser welded joints of titanium alloy.
In the present study, the aim was to investigate the microstructure
and mechanical properties of 3 mm thick TC4 titanium alloy laser
welded joints and obtain the optimal welding parameters.
The micro -
source.relevance_reason: The chunk mentions a laser welding study by Emre E H et al. that reported a welding speed of 250 cm/min and the resulting maximum tensile strength, which are directly relevant target parameters.

source.chunk:
structure, fracture morphology, grain orientation, grain misorientation
and grain size of laser welded joints for optimal welding parameters
were analyzed in detail.
The results of this paper could provide a deep
understanding of the laser welding process of TC4 titanium alloy.
2. Materials and experimental procedures
2.1. Materials and laser
Annealed plates of Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy were used in this study
with a thickness of 3 mm.
Ti-6Al-4V is a typical alpha – beta phase tita -
nium alloy.
The chemical composition of the base metal is given in
Table 1 .
For the welding procedure, titanium alloy plates with dimensions of
80 mm × 75 mm × 3 mm were made. IPG Photonics, a German com -
pany, produced the IPG YLS-10000 fiber laser with 10 kW laser power
and 470 mm focal length.
As shown in Fig.
1 , a KUKA-KP-C4 robot, robot
control system and YLS-10000 fiber laser made up the laser.
Stand-up
axes were used in conjunction with the KUKA-KP-C4 robot.
2.2. Pre-weld treatment and welding
Coarse sandpaper was used to polish the welded surface of plates
made of the Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy before performing welding trials.
The specimens were then etched for approximately 120 s using 90 ml
pure water + 5 ml HNO
+ 5 ml HF to remove surface stains.
Finally, the
surface of the specimens was wiped with dehydrated ethanol and
allowed to dry.
These treated specimens were then welded as soon as
possible.
Following program selection, the robot control system controlled the
laser power, welding speed, and defocused position, while the experi -
ments were conducted under obverse and reverse protection of pure
argon gas.
The flow rates for the obverse and reverse shielding gases
were 25 L/min and 15 L/min, respectively.
The 3 mm thick Ti-6Al-4V
titanium alloy plates were welded in the butt configuration.
Parame -
ters were explored based on the results of previous self-fusion welding
trials, the welding parameters used in this study are given in Table 2 .
The L9(3
) orthogonal experimental scheme is given in Table 3 .
2.3. Tensile tests
Calculating the dimensions of a 3 mm thick plate following the
tensile test method on welded joints (GB/T 228.1 – 2021), the tensile
specimens with the dimensions presented in Fig.
2 were used to gather
information on the tensile characteristics of the weldments.
Wire-cutting
was performed using electrical discharge machining (EDM) on a set of
three tensile specimens.
An SHT-4605 type of microcomputer-controlled
electronic universal testing machine with a 58 kN capacity and a 1 mm/
min elongation rate was used to test all tensile specimens for different
welding parameters.
Following tensile tests, a HITACHI S-3400 SEM was
used to examine the fracture morphology of the specimens.
2.4. Microstructure investigation
The metallographic specimens were polished with grinding discs
until the surface exhibited a mirror-like finish and then electrolytically
polished using a polishing agent (10 ml perchloric acid + 30 ml n-butyl
alcohol + 60 ml methyl alcohol).
Next, the surfaces of metallographic
specimens were etched using 90 ml pure water + 6 ml HNO
+ 4 ml HF.
The OM was used to observe the important aspects of the metallurgical

parameters obtained through the analysis of range and variance were
laser power 2.3 kW, welding speed 0.04 m/s, and defocused position 0
mm.
Moreover, the optimal welding parameters were used as the
welding parameters for sample 10.
3.2. Microstructure of the laser welded joints
Fig.
3 shows the microstructure of metallographic specimen 10.
According to the temperature of different regions of the welded joint in
the welding process, the welded joint was divided into the BM, HAZ and
FZ.
As shown in Fig.
3 (a), the HAZ and FZ differed significantly from the
BM.
The microstructure of the BM is displayed in Fig. 3 (b).
The dark-
colored β phase (body centered cubic) was dispersed in the light-
colored α matrix (hexagonal close packed).
Fig.
3 (c) shows the microstructure of the HAZ.
A phase transition in
the HAZ occurred without melting of the welded joint.
The micro -
structure was composed of the α phase and β phase at high temperatures.
During the rapid cooling process, with little variation in the α phase and
β phase, the crystallographic structure changed from body centered
cubic to hexagonal close packed [24,25] .
The HAZ consisted of a small
amount of α ′ martensite, predominantly α
and β
As the temperature in
the HAZ reached the α phase and β phase transformation temperature,
some of the α phase and β phase transformed into the high-temperature β
phase.
Because this temperature was not high, the high-temperature β
phase did not grow, and little of the high-temperature β phase trans -
formed into the secondary α ′ phase during the post-weld cooling process.
As shown in Fig.
3 (d), compared with the acicular α ′ martensite far from
the FZ, the acicular α ′ martensite became coarser and denser close to the
FZ.
Because the closer the FZ was, the higher the temperature, the more
high-temperature β phase formed.
Furthermore, more martensite was
formed by transformation during cooling.
Compared to the FZ, the
undercooling and the temperature of the HAZ were lower, and thus the
α ′ martensite domains in the HAZ were fewer and finer.
The microstructure of the FZ is given in Fig.
3 (e), and the FZ was
characterized by epitaxial solidification.
The grains in the FZ were based
on semi-melted grains as the nucleation mechanism extended to the
